title: Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Act 2006
chapters: 62 sections in 3 Parts. Part 1 (ss.1-14): Appeals and removal. Part 2 (ss.15-26): Employment — the employer obligations framework. Part 3 (ss.27-62): Information, claimants andடுother matters. Employment provisions (ss.15-25) are the core employer-facing provisions, supplemented by the Immigration (Restrictions on Employment) Order 2007 (SI 2007/3290), the Code of Practice on Preventing Illegal Working (13 February 2024), and the Home Office Employer Right to Work Guidance (updated 26 June 2025).

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Immigration, Asylum and Nationality Bill [Government Bill]
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — introduced by the Labour Government, Home Secretary Charles Clarke (later John Reid)
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Royal Assent 30 March 2006 but the employment provisions (ss.15-25) did not come into force until 29 February 2008 (SI 2008/310). This delay allowed the Home Office to develop the Code of Practice, the prescribed document lists, and employer guidance before the penalty regime became enforceable. The Act replaced the previous employer sanctions regime under the Asylum and Immigration Act 1996 s.8.

significantAmendments.effect: Increased maximum civil penalty from £10,000 to £20,000 per illegal worker. Introduced the online right to work checking service. Extended the scope of prescribed documents. Strengthened enforcement powers.

CRITICAL amendments: (1) Extended the criminal offence under s.21 from 'knowing' to 'knowing OR having reasonable cause to believe' — significantly lowering the criminal threshold. (2) Increased maximum imprisonment from 2 years to 5 years on indictment. (3) Created a new employee offence of illegal working (Immigration Act 1971 s.24B). (4) Extended powers for Immigration Officers to enter premises, search for documents, and seize evidence.

significantAmendments.instrument: Code of Practice on Preventing Illegal Working (13 February 2024)
significantAmendments.effect: CURRENT Code. Tripled civil penalty starting points: first breach £45,000 per worker (was £15,000); repeat breach £60,000 per worker (was £20,000). Introduced fast payment option (30% reduction within 21 days for first breach only). Updated prescribed document lists. Applies to all breaches occurring on or after 13 February 2024.

significantAmendments.instrument: Home Office Employer Right to Work Guidance (updated 26 June 2025)
significantAmendments.effect: Current guidance on conducting right to work checks. Three approved methods: manual document check, online check (share code), and IDVT (Identity Document Validation Technology via certified IDSP). Applies to all checks carried out on or after 12 February 2025.

precedingDocuments.title: Asylum and Immigration Act 1996 s.8 (replaced regime)
precedingDocuments.significance: The previous employer sanctions scheme. s.23 of the 2006 Act abolished s.8. The 1996 scheme was widely criticised as ineffective — penalties were rarely imposed and the documentary checks were inadequate. The 2006 Act was designed to create a more robust and enforceable regime.

precedingDocuments.title: Controlling our Borders: Making Migration Work for Britain (Five Year Strategy, Cm 6472, February 2005)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Government's five-year immigration strategy that set the policy context for the 2006 Act. It identified illegal working as a key driver of irregular migration and proposed strengthened employer sanctions.

precedingDocuments.title: Code of Practice on Avoiding Race Discrimination while Preventing Illegal Working
precedingDocuments.significance: Statutory Code requiring employers to conduct right to work checks on ALL prospective employees equally, regardless of race, nationality, ethnic origin, or appearance. Failure to check all candidates equally may constitute race discrimination under the Equality Act 2010.

leadingCases.name: No single UKSC/UKHL leading case on the civil penalty regime
leadingCases.principle: The IANA 2006 civil penalty regime has not produced a landmark Supreme Court or House of Lords decision. Challenges to civil penalties are typically dealt with at county court level (s.17 appeals) or by way of judicial review of Home Office decisions. The absence of higher court authority reflects the administrative nature of the regime — most disputes are resolved through the objection (s.16) and appeal (s.17) process without reaching the appellate courts.
leadingCases.significance: SURVEILLANCE — Eileen must monitor for significant county court and Administrative Court decisions on the scope of 'statutory excuse', the meaning of 'reasonable cause to believe' under s.21, and challenges to the 2024 penalty levels.

parts.title: Part 1 — The Civil Penalty Regime: s.15 and the Employer's Strict Liability

sections.title: Civil penalty for employing an adult subject to immigration control — strict liability unless statutory excuse
sections.summary: s.15 creates a civil penalty regime of extraordinary severity. An employer is liable to a penalty if they employ a person aged 16 or over who is subject to immigration control and who: (a) has not been granted leave to enter or remain in the UK, or (b) whose leave is invalid, has ceased to have effect, or is subject to a condition preventing the employment. This is STRICT LIABILITY — the employer's intention or knowledge is irrelevant. The employer does NOT need to have known the employee lacked permission to work. The only defence is the STATUTORY EXCUSE: if the employer conducted the prescribed right to work checks correctly before employment commenced, they are excused from the penalty (s.15(3)). But even the statutory excuse is lost if the employer KNEW the employment was unlawful (s.15(4)).
sections.text:
s.15(1): It is contrary to this section to employ an adult subject to immigration control if—
(a) he has not been granted leave to enter or remain in the United Kingdom, or
(b) his leave to enter or remain in the United Kingdom—
(i) is invalid,
(ii) has ceased to have effect (whether by reason of curtailment, revocation, cancellation, passage of time or otherwise), or
(iii) is subject to a condition preventing him from accepting the employment.

s.15(2): The Secretary of State may give an employer who acts contrary to subsection (1) a notice requiring him to pay a penalty of a specified amount not exceeding the prescribed maximum.

s.15(3): An employer is excused from paying a penalty if the employer has complied with any prescribed requirements in relation to the employment.

s.15(4): But the excuse in subsection (3) shall not apply to an employer who knew, at any time during the period of the employment, that it was contrary to this section.

PENALTY LEVELS (Code of Practice, 13 February 2024):
— First breach: £45,000 per illegal worker (starting point)
— Repeat breach (within 3 years): £60,000 per illegal worker (starting point)
— Previous levels (breaches before 13 February 2024): £15,000 first / £20,000 repeat
— Fast Payment Option: 30% reduction if paid within 21 days (first breach only)

Mitigating factors that may reduce the penalty:
(a) Reporting: employer reported the suspected illegal worker to the Home Office
(b) Active cooperation: employer cooperated fully and promptly with the investigation
(c) Effective systems: employer can demonstrate robust right to work checking procedures were in place even if the specific check was flawed

The penalty is calculated PER WORKER — an employer found to have employed 5 illegal workers faces a potential starting point of £225,000 (first breach) or £300,000 (repeat breach).
sections.keyPrinciple: This is a STRICT LIABILITY regime with an EXCUSATORY DEFENCE. The employer does not need to have intended to employ an illegal worker — liability arises from the bare fact of employment without permission. The ONLY defence is the statutory excuse (correct prescribed checks). Knowledge destroys even the statutory excuse. The penalty levels from February 2024 are punitive: £45,000 per worker first breach, £60,000 per worker repeat. For a business employing even a small number of illegal workers, the financial exposure is devastating.
refs: Immigration (Restrictions on Employment) Order 2007 (SI 2007/3290) — prescribed documents and check requirements
Code of Practice on Preventing Illegal Working (13 February 2024) — current Code
Home Office Employer Right to Work Guidance (updated 26 June 2025)
s.16 (objection to penalty — 28 days)
s.17 (appeal to county court — 28 days from objection decision)
s.21 (criminal offence — separate and additional to civil penalty)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice on Preventing Illegal Working (13 February 2024)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The governing Code that determines how penalties are calculated, what mitigating factors apply, and the distinction between first and repeat breaches. This is the primary reference document for all employer right to work obligations.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Right to work checks: an employer's guide (updated 26 June 2025)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Detailed guidance on conducting each of the three approved check methods, the prescribed document lists, and when follow-up checks are required.

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice on Avoiding Race Discrimination while Preventing Illegal Working
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Statutory Code requiring employers to check ALL prospective employees equally. Selective checking based on appearance, accent, name, or perceived nationality is race discrimination under the EqA 2010.

commonErrors: Failing to conduct right to work checks BEFORE employment commences — the statutory excuse requires the check to be completed BEFORE the first day of work. A check conducted after employment has started provides no statutory excuse for the period before the check

Conducting checks on some employees but not others based on appearance, accent, or perceived nationality — this is race discrimination under the EqA 2010 and violates the Code of Practice on Avoiding Race Discrimination. ALL prospective employees must be checked equally

Assuming that a British-sounding name or local accent means no check is needed — the right to work check must be conducted on EVERY new employee regardless of apparent nationality. Only British citizens, Irish citizens, and those with settled status have unconditional right to work, and this must still be verified through prescribed documents

Failing to conduct follow-up checks for employees with time-limited permission — where an employee's right to work is time-limited (e.g., visa with expiry date), the employer must conduct a follow-up check before the permission expires to maintain the statutory excuse

Not retaining copies of the documents checked — the statutory excuse requires the employer to retain copies (physical or electronic) of the documents for the duration of employment PLUS two years after employment ends

Treating the penalty as a fixed amount — the penalty is a STARTING POINT that can be reduced through mitigating factors (reporting, cooperation, effective systems) or increased for repeat breaches. Employers should always assess mitigation before paying

Paying the penalty without considering objection or appeal — employers have 28 days to object (s.16) and a further 28 days to appeal to the county court (s.17). The fast payment option (30% reduction) applies only to first breaches

versions.text: Original penalty: up to £10,000 per illegal worker.
versions.policyRationale: The original penalty level was set at £10,000 to provide a meaningful deterrent while allowing for the regime to be tested before escalation.

versions.text: Maximum penalty increased to £20,000. Code of Practice levels: £15,000 first breach, £20,000 repeat.
versions.policyRationale: Doubled to reflect inflation and to strengthen the deterrent effect.

versions.text: CURRENT: Code of Practice levels tripled — £45,000 first breach, £60,000 repeat breach per worker.
versions.amendedBy: Code of Practice on Preventing Illegal Working (13 February 2024)
versions.policyRationale: Government tripled penalty levels as part of a broader crackdown on illegal working. The stated objective was to make the financial cost of non-compliance exceed the financial benefit of employing illegal workers.

parts.title: Part 2 — The Statutory Excuse: Right to Work Checks in Practice

sections.num: s.15(3) + Immigration (Restrictions on Employment) Order 2007 + Home Office Guidance 2025
sections.title: Establishing and maintaining the statutory excuse — the three approved check methods
sections.summary: The statutory excuse is the employer's ONLY defence against a civil penalty. It requires the employer to conduct a prescribed right to work check using one of three approved methods BEFORE employment begins, and to retain evidence of the check. The three methods are: (1) MANUAL CHECK — inspect original documents from the prescribed list in the physical or virtual presence of the holder, confirm they are genuine, belong to the person, and allow the work offered, then take and retain a clear copy. (2) ONLINE CHECK — use the Home Office online right to work checking service with the employee's share code and date of birth. This produces a real-time immigration status result. Retain a clear copy of the online profile page. (3) IDVT (Identity Document Validation Technology) — use a certified Identity Service Provider (IDSP) to verify a valid British or Irish passport (including expired Irish passports). This produces a digitally verified result. The statutory excuse is CONTINUOUS for employees with permanent right to work (British/Irish citizens, settled status). It is TIME-LIMITED for employees with temporary permission — the employer must conduct a follow-up check before the permission expires.
sections.text:
THREE APPROVED CHECK METHODS (Home Office Guidance, 26 June 2025):

METHOD 1 — MANUAL DOCUMENT CHECK:
(a) Obtain original documents from List A (permanent RTW) or List B (time-limited RTW) in the prescribed lists.
(b) Check the documents in the presence of the holder (physical or live video).
(c) Satisfy yourself that the documents are genuine, relate to the person presenting them, and allow the person to do the work offered.
(d) Take a clear copy (scan or photograph) of the relevant pages.
(e) Record the date the check was made.
(f) Retain the copies securely for the duration of employment plus 2 years.

METHOD 2 — ONLINE CHECK:
(a) The employee provides a share code generated via the Home Office online right to work service.
(b) Enter the share code and the employee's date of birth at gov.uk/check-worker-right-to-work.
(c) The service provides a real-time result confirming or denying the right to work.
(d) Print or save a copy of the online profile page as evidence.
(e) Record the date the check was made.
(f) Retain the evidence securely.

Online checks are MANDATORY for: holders of eVisas, holders of Biometric Residence Permits (BRPs) from 2025 onwards, EU/EEA citizens with settled or pre-settled status (these individuals cannot use manual document checks — they MUST use the online service).

METHOD 3 — IDVT (Identity Document Validation Technology):
(a) Use a certified Identity Service Provider (IDSP) to verify a valid British or Irish passport.
(b) Available ONLY for British and Irish passport holders.
(c) The IDSP conducts digital identity verification and produces a verification result.
(d) Retain evidence of the IDSP result.

FOLLOW-UP CHECKS:
For employees with time-limited permission (List B documents), the employer must conduct a follow-up check before the permission expires. If the employee is awaiting a Home Office decision on a pending application, the employer may use the Employer Checking Service (ECS) to verify their status. A positive ECS response provides a statutory excuse for 6 months.

PRESCRIBED DOCUMENT LISTS:

LIST A — Establishes a CONTINUOUS statutory excuse (permanent right to work):
Acceptable documents include:
(a) A British passport (current or expired) confirming British citizenship or right of abode
(b) An Irish passport or passport card (current or expired)
(c) A certificate of registration or naturalisation as a British citizen
(d) A permanent residence card, an indefinite leave to remain (ILR) stamp/vignette, or a Biometric Residence Permit showing ILR
(e) An online right to work check confirming settled status (EU Settlement Scheme) or indefinite leave
(f) A birth or adoption certificate issued in the UK, Channel Islands, Isle of Man, or Ireland COMBINED with an official document showing the holder's NI number (e.g., P45, P60, NI card)

LIST B — Establishes a TIME-LIMITED statutory excuse (temporary right to work):
Acceptable documents include:
(a) A current passport with a valid visa or entry clearance endorsing the holder's right to work
(b) A current BRP showing limited leave with permission to work
(c) A current Immigration Status Document with endorsement for work
(d) An online right to work check confirming pre-settled status or limited leave
(e) A positive verification from the Employer Checking Service (ECS) — provides a 6-month statutory excuse, renewable

Note: The full prescribed lists are updated periodically by the Home Office. Employers must consult the CURRENT version of the Right to Work Guidance for the complete and up-to-date lists.

WHEN AN EMPLOYEE LOSES THEIR RIGHT TO WORK DURING EMPLOYMENT:

(1) If a follow-up check reveals the employee's permission has expired and no further permission has been granted:
— The employer must NOT continue to employ the person
— Continued employment after the employer becomes aware (or ought reasonably to be aware) that permission has expired creates fresh s.15 liability from the date of awareness
— The employer should give the employee an opportunity to regularise their status (e.g., make a new visa application) but CANNOT continue to employ them in the meantime unless an ECS check confirms ongoing permission

(2) If the employee has a pending application made BEFORE the previous permission expired (s.3C leave — Immigration Act 1971):
— The employee's conditions of leave continue on the same basis until the application is decided
— The employer should use the ECS to confirm this — a positive ECS response provides a 6-month statutory excuse
— The employer should NOT dismiss the employee without first checking ECS — dismissal without checking may constitute unfair dismissal

(3) If the employer discovers the employee NEVER had permission to work:
— Employment must cease immediately
— The employer should report the matter to the Home Office (mitigating factor for penalty reduction)
— The employer should seek legal advice on the dismissal process to avoid unfair dismissal and race discrimination claims

'REASONABLY APPARENT' FAKE DOCUMENT TEST:

The statutory excuse is lost if it was 'reasonably apparent' that the document was not genuine, did not belong to the holder, or did not permit the work. The standard is that of a REASONABLE EMPLOYER, not an immigration expert. Indicators that should put a reasonable employer on notice include:
(a) The photograph does not match the person presenting the document
(b) The document has been visibly altered, damaged, or tampered with
(c) The document is in a format that does not match known genuine documents
(d) The name on the document does not match the name given by the person
(e) The dates do not make sense (e.g., date of birth making the person impossibly young or old)
(f) The document is a photocopy or digital reproduction rather than an original
sections.keyPrinciple: The statutory excuse is a structured, prescriptive, and unforgiving framework. Every step must be followed precisely. A check that is substantively correct but procedurally flawed (e.g., documents checked but not copied, or copies not retained) may fail to establish a statutory excuse. The employer bears the burden of proving the excuse — if the Home Office challenges it, the employer must produce the evidence.
refs: Immigration (Restrictions on Employment) Order 2007 (SI 2007/3290)
Home Office Right to Work Checks: An Employer's Guide (updated 26 June 2025)
Code of Practice on Preventing Illegal Working (13 February 2024)
Home Office Employer Checking Service (ECS) — for pending applications
IDSP certification requirements
commonErrors: Accepting photocopies, scanned documents, or emailed copies instead of inspecting ORIGINAL documents in the holder's presence — the manual check requires inspection of the ORIGINAL document, not a copy sent by the employee

Using the manual document check method for EU/EEA citizens with settled or pre-settled status — these individuals MUST be checked using the online service (share code). Their physical documents (BRP, EU passport) cannot be used for a manual right to work check from 2025 onwards

Failing to check that the document allows the SPECIFIC WORK offered — a visa with a restriction (e.g., no more than 20 hours per week, or a specific employer named) must be checked against the actual work offered. Employing someone in breach of their visa conditions engages s.15 even if the person has some permission to work

Not conducting follow-up checks when an employee's time-limited permission is about to expire — the statutory excuse expires when the permission expires. If the employer continues to employ the person after expiry without a new check, liability arises from the date of expiry

Relying on an expired BRP or vignette as proof of right to work — from 2025, the Home Office has transitioned to eVisas. Physical immigration documents (BRPs) may be expired but the underlying immigration permission may still be valid. The online checking service must be used to verify current status

Not using the Employer Checking Service (ECS) when an employee's permission expires but they have a pending application — a positive ECS response provides a statutory excuse for 6 months, renewable. Dismissing an employee without checking ECS may expose the employer to unfair dismissal claims

Delegating right to work checks to an external recruitment agency without retaining responsibility — the employer remains legally liable even if the agency conducted the check. Best practice is for the employer to conduct its own check OR to obtain confirmation and copies from the agency

Not knowing which documents are on List A (permanent RTW) vs List B (time-limited RTW) — an employer who accepts a List B document without scheduling a follow-up check loses the statutory excuse when the permission expires

Continuing to employ someone after discovering their visa has expired without checking the Employer Checking Service — if the employee has a pending application, s.3C leave may continue their permission. Dismissing without checking ECS may be unfair dismissal AND race discrimination

Accepting documents that a reasonable employer should recognise as fake — the 'reasonably apparent' test does not require immigration expertise, but it does require basic vigilance: checking the photograph matches, the document appears genuine, and the details are consistent

Failing to check ALL employees including zero-hours workers, casual staff, and fixed-term contract workers — the right to work obligation applies to EVERY person employed under a contract of employment or apprenticeship, regardless of contract type or hours

parts.title: Part 3 — The Criminal Offence: s.21 — Knowingly Employing an Illegal Worker

sections.num: s.21 (as amended by Immigration Act 2016 s.35)
sections.title: Criminal offence of employing an illegal worker — knowing or having reasonable cause to believe
sections.summary: s.21 creates a CRIMINAL offence, separate from and additional to the civil penalty under s.15. As amended by the Immigration Act 2016, an employer commits an offence if they employ a person knowing OR having reasonable cause to believe that the person is disqualified from working by reason of their immigration status. The 2016 amendment was critical: it lowered the threshold from 'knowing' (very difficult to prove) to 'knowing or having reasonable cause to believe' (much easier to prove). The maximum penalty on indictment is 5 years' imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine. Company directors, managers, and company secretaries can be prosecuted personally under s.22 if they consented to or connived in the offence. The statutory excuse does NOT protect against criminal liability — if the employer knew or had reasonable cause to believe, no amount of documentary checking can save them.
sections.text:
s.21(1) (as amended): A person commits an offence if the person—
(a) employs another person ('the employee') who is an adult subject to immigration control, and
(b) at that time the person knows or has reasonable cause to believe that the employee is disqualified from employment by reason of the employee's immigration status.

s.21(1A) (inserted by IA 2016): A person commits an offence if the person—
(a) employs another person ('the employee') who is disqualified from employment by reason of the employee's immigration status, and
(b) at the time of employing the employee, has reasonable cause to believe that the employee is disqualified from employment by reason of the employee's immigration status.

Maximum penalties:
— On indictment: 5 years' imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine.
— Summary conviction: 12 months' imprisonment and/or a fine (England and Wales).

s.22 — Officer liability:
Where an offence under s.21 is committed by a body corporate with the consent or connivance of an officer (director, manager, company secretary), the officer is also guilty of the offence and liable to prosecution personally.

'Reasonable cause to believe' includes:
(a) Ignoring expired visa documents without checking renewal status
(b) Failing to follow up on alerts about visa expiry dates
(c) Disregarding information from colleagues, employees, or third parties about an employee's immigration status
(d) Employing someone who cannot produce ANY immigration documents and failing to check via the Employer Checking Service
(e) A pattern of employing workers from high-risk sectors without conducting any checks
sections.keyPrinciple: The 2016 amendment transformed s.21 from a rarely-prosecuted offence (because 'knowing' was almost impossible to prove) into a genuinely enforceable criminal sanction. 'Reasonable cause to believe' is a significantly lower threshold — it captures employers who turn a blind eye, ignore red flags, or fail to follow up on expired documents. Since 2025, the Home Office has increased criminal referrals for repeat offenders who continue to employ illegal workers despite previous civil penalties. Company directors face personal prosecution.
refs: Immigration Act 2016 s.35 (amended s.21 — 'reasonable cause to believe' inserted)
Immigration Act 2016 s.34 (created employee offence — Immigration Act 1971 s.24B)
IANA 2006 s.22 (officer liability — directors, managers, company secretaries)
IANA 2006 s.15 (civil penalty — separate and additional regime)
Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986 (disqualification following conviction)
commonErrors: Assuming that conducting right to work checks provides a defence against criminal prosecution — the statutory excuse protects against CIVIL penalties only. It does NOT protect against CRIMINAL liability where the employer knew or had reasonable cause to believe the employee was working illegally

Failing to recognise that 'reasonable cause to believe' is a LOW threshold — ignoring expired visa documents, failing to follow up on expiry alerts, or disregarding information about an employee's status can all constitute reasonable cause to believe

Company directors assuming they cannot be prosecuted personally — s.22 imposes PERSONAL criminal liability on directors, managers, and company secretaries who consent to or connive in the offence. Conviction can lead to imprisonment, unlimited fine, AND director disqualification

Treating a civil penalty as the end of the matter — a civil penalty can trigger a criminal investigation. Evidence gathered in the civil process (documents, interview notes, internal correspondence) can be used in the criminal prosecution. Internal communications showing awareness of immigration issues should be treated as potentially disclosable

Since 2025, the Home Office has escalated criminal referrals for repeat offenders — employers who receive multiple civil penalties are increasingly being referred for criminal investigation under s.21

versions.text: Original s.21: offence of KNOWINGLY employing an illegal worker. Maximum: 2 years' imprisonment and/or unlimited fine. Rarely prosecuted because 'knowing' was almost impossible to prove beyond reasonable doubt.
versions.policyRationale: Original threshold was deliberately high — the Government intended the civil penalty to be the primary enforcement mechanism with criminal prosecution reserved for the most egregious cases.

versions.text: CURRENT: Extended to 'knowing OR having reasonable cause to believe'. Maximum: 5 years' imprisonment and/or unlimited fine. Criminal referrals increasing since 2025.
versions.policyRationale: The Government lowered the criminal threshold because the original 'knowing' standard was too difficult to prove, undermining the deterrent effect of the criminal offence.

sections.title: Objection to penalty, appeal to county court, and debt recovery
sections.summary: The employer has two routes to challenge a civil penalty: objection (s.16) to the Secretary of State within 28 days, then appeal (s.17) to the county court within 28 days of the objection decision. The grounds for objection are limited: (a) the employer is not liable (the person had permission to work), (b) the employer has a statutory excuse (prescribed checks were correctly conducted), or (c) the penalty amount is too high (the Code of Practice was misapplied). If no objection or appeal is made, the penalty becomes enforceable as a civil debt (s.20) — recoverable through county court procedures including attachment of earnings, charging orders, and warrants of control.
sections.text:
s.16 — Objection:
(1) The employer may object to the penalty within 28 days of the penalty notice.
(2) The objection must be in writing and must set out the grounds.
(3) The Secretary of State must consider the objection and either cancel, reduce, or confirm the penalty.
(4) The decision must be notified to the employer.

s.17 — Appeal:
(1) The employer may appeal to the county court (or Sheriff Court in Scotland) within 28 days of receiving the objection decision.
(2) The court may cancel, reduce, increase, or confirm the penalty.
(3) The court's decision is final.

s.20 — Recovery:
(1) An unpaid penalty is recoverable as a civil debt.
(2) Interest accrues on unpaid penalties.
(3) The Home Office may pursue county court enforcement, including attachment of earnings and warrant of control.
(4) Unpaid penalties are published on the Home Office quarterly penalty list.

Timelines:
— Day 0: Civil Penalty Notice issued
— Day 21: Fast Payment Option expires (30% reduction for first breach only)
— Day 28: Objection deadline
— Day 56+ (approx): Objection decision received
— Day 84+ (approx): Appeal deadline (28 days from objection decision)
— If no payment, objection, or appeal: debt recovery and enforcement

INFORMATION REQUEST STAGE:

Before issuing a Civil Penalty Notice, the Home Office typically follows this process:
(1) Encounter or intelligence — Immigration Enforcement identifies a potential illegal worker (e.g., through a compliance visit, raid, data match, or tip-off).
(2) Referral Notice — the employer is informed that a potential breach has been identified.
(3) Information Request — the employer has 10 WORKING DAYS to submit evidence including copies of right to work checks, recruitment records, and any documents that may establish a statutory excuse or mitigation.
(4) Assessment — the Home Office assesses the evidence and determines whether to issue a penalty, a warning notice, or take no further action.
(5) Civil Penalty Notice — issued if the Home Office concludes that the employer is liable.

The Information Request stage is CRITICAL — failure to respond, or responding inadequately, may result in the maximum penalty being imposed without mitigation.

TUPE AND RIGHT TO WORK CHECKS:

Where a business transfers under TUPE (Transfer of Undertakings (Protection of Employment) Regulations 2006), the new employer inherits the existing employment contracts. The Home Office guidance states that the new employer does NOT need to repeat the right to work check if the previous employer conducted a valid check — but should obtain copies of the check records from the transferor. However, if the new employer cannot obtain the records, they should conduct a fresh check to establish their own statutory excuse.

EMPLOYMENT AGENCIES (s.24):

Employment agencies and businesses that supply workers are subject to specific obligations. The agency must conduct right to work checks on the workers it supplies. However, the END USER (the employer who actually engages the worker) also retains responsibility if the worker is employed under a contract of employment. Where a worker is supplied by an agency under a contract for services (not a contract of employment), the agency bears primary responsibility — but the end user should exercise due diligence.
sections.keyPrinciple: The deadlines are SHORT and STRICT. Missing the 28-day objection deadline means the penalty becomes final. Missing the appeal deadline means the county court cannot hear the case. Employers who receive a civil penalty notice must seek legal advice immediately — the clock starts running on the date of the notice, not the date it is received.
refs: s.16 (objection — 28 days)
s.17 (appeal — 28 days from objection decision)
s.20 (recovery as civil debt)
Code of Practice (penalty calculation methodology)
commonErrors: Missing the 28-day objection deadline — the penalty becomes enforceable as a civil debt if no objection is made within 28 days. There is no provision for late objections

Paying the penalty without objecting when legitimate grounds exist — the fast payment option (30% reduction) is attractive but should not be accepted without first assessing whether the employer has a valid statutory excuse or other defence

Not seeking legal advice immediately upon receiving a civil penalty notice — the 21-day fast payment window and 28-day objection deadline leave very little time. Professional advice on whether to pay, object, or seek a review should be obtained within the first 7 days
Ignoring the penalty and hoping it will go away — unpaid penalties are pursued as civil debts, published on the Home Office quarterly list, and may trigger sponsor licence action

Failing to respond to the Information Request within 10 working days — this is the employer's best opportunity to submit evidence of a statutory excuse or mitigating factors BEFORE the penalty is imposed. Non-response typically results in the maximum penalty

Assuming that a TUPE transfer automatically transfers the statutory excuse — the new employer should obtain copies of the previous employer's right to work check records. If records are unavailable, a fresh check should be conducted

Employment agencies assuming the end-user client bears all responsibility for right to work — where the agency employs the worker, the agency bears primary responsibility for conducting checks. Both the agency and the end user should conduct checks to protect their respective positions

parts.title: Part 5 — The Discrimination Intersection: Right to Work Checks and the Equality Act 2010

sections.num: Cross-reference: EqA 2010 + Code of Practice on Avoiding Race Discrimination
sections.title: Conducting right to work checks without discriminating — the mandatory balancing act
sections.summary: Employers face a mandatory balancing act: they MUST conduct right to work checks on all prospective employees (IANA 2006 s.15), but they must NOT discriminate in how they conduct those checks (EqA 2010 s.13 — direct race discrimination, s.19 — indirect race discrimination). The Home Office Code of Practice on Avoiding Race Discrimination while Preventing Illegal Working is a statutory code that requires employers to: treat all prospective employees in the same way regardless of race, colour, nationality, ethnic or national origins; not make assumptions about a person's right to work based on appearance, accent, name, or perceived nationality; check the same documents for all candidates; and not treat candidates with foreign-sounding names or non-British appearance differently. An employer who conducts right to work checks only on employees who 'look foreign' commits race discrimination — even if the check reveals an employee who genuinely lacks permission to work.
sections.text:
THE DISCRIMINATION TRAP:

(1) LEGAL OBLIGATION: Check ALL prospective employees' right to work (IANA 2006 s.15).
(2) LEGAL PROHIBITION: Do not discriminate on grounds of race in conducting checks (EqA 2010 s.13/s.19).
(3) THE SOLUTION: Check EVERYONE equally, using the same process, at the same stage of recruitment.

Practical implementation:
(a) Embed right to work checks into the standard onboarding process — conducted for EVERY new starter at the same point (e.g., after conditional offer, before first day).
(b) Use the same document verification process for all candidates.
(c) Do not ask questions about nationality, immigration status, or origin at interview stage — these are checked during the formal right to work process.
(d) Do not refuse to interview or shortlist candidates based on perceived nationality or accent.
(e) If a candidate cannot provide the required documents, follow up with the Home Office Employer Checking Service before making employment decisions.
(f) Train all hiring managers on the non-discrimination obligation alongside the right to work obligation.

Key EqA 2010 provisions:
— s.13: Direct discrimination — treating someone less favourably because of race (including nationality and ethnic origin).
— s.19: Indirect discrimination — applying a provision, criterion, or practice that puts people of a particular racial group at a disadvantage.
— s.39(1): Discrimination in recruitment — making arrangements for deciding to whom to offer employment.
sections.keyPrinciple: The employer is caught between two legal obligations: the duty to check (IANA 2006) and the duty not to discriminate (EqA 2010). The only safe path is UNIVERSAL, CONSISTENT, NON-DISCRIMINATORY checking of ALL prospective employees at the same stage of the process. Selective checking is illegal. Not checking is illegal. Only universal checking is lawful.
refs: EqA 2010 s.13 (direct race discrimination)
EqA 2010 s.19 (indirect race discrimination)
EqA 2010 s.39(1) (discrimination in recruitment)
Code of Practice on Avoiding Race Discrimination while Preventing Illegal Working
EHRC Code of Practice on Employment (race discrimination in recruitment)
commonErrors: Conducting right to work checks only on employees who appear foreign, have non-British accents, or have foreign-sounding names — this is direct race discrimination under EqA 2010 s.13, regardless of whether the check reveals an employee without permission to work

Asking candidates about their nationality, visa status, or immigration history at interview — right to work is verified through the formal checking process AFTER a conditional offer, not through interview questions
Applying different document requirements to employees of different nationalities — the same prescribed check process must be applied to all candidates

Refusing to employ someone because they provided a non-British passport even though the passport confirms right to work — a valid passport with a valid visa or settled status stamp is a prescribed document regardless of the issuing country

Dismissing an employee when their right to work expires without first checking the Employer Checking Service — if the employee has a pending application, they may have a continued right to work. Dismissing without checking may constitute both unfair dismissal (ERA 1996) and race discrimination (EqA 2010)

parts.title: Part 6 — Sponsor Licence and Wider Consequences

sections.num: Cross-reference: Immigration Rules + sponsor licence framework
sections.title: Wider consequences of a civil penalty — sponsor licence, reputation, and operational impact
sections.summary: A civil penalty for illegal working has consequences far beyond the financial penalty itself. For employers who hold a sponsor licence (required to employ Skilled Workers, Global Business Mobility, and other sponsored categories), a civil penalty almost always triggers a UKVI review of the licence. The licence may be suspended (preventing new sponsorship applications), downgraded (from A-rated to B-rated, requiring an action plan), or revoked (terminating all existing sponsorships — sponsored workers' visas may be curtailed, requiring them to find a new sponsor or leave the UK within 60 days). For all employers, the Home Office may publish the employer's name on the quarterly penalty list. Media coverage, client scrutiny, and regulatory consequences in sectors such as care, financial services, and education can cause lasting reputational damage. Directors may face personal disqualification. The total cost of a civil penalty — financial penalty + legal costs + sponsor licence action + reputational damage + operational disruption — typically far exceeds the penalty amount itself.
sections.text:
ESCALATION CASCADE:

(1) Civil Penalty Notice issued
(2) If sponsor licence held:
    → UKVI compliance review triggered
    → Licence may be suspended (no new sponsorships)
    → Licence may be downgraded to B-rating (action plan required)
    → Licence may be REVOKED:
       — All existing Certificates of Sponsorship cancelled
       — Sponsored workers' visas may be curtailed
       — Workers must find new sponsor within 60 days or leave UK
       — Employer cannot reapply for licence for 12 months (cooling-off period)
(3) If repeat breach or aggravating factors:
    → Criminal investigation under s.21
    → Director personal prosecution under s.22
    → Company Directors Disqualification Act proceedings
(4) For all employers:
    → Publication on quarterly penalty list
    → Media coverage and reputational damage
    → Client and investor scrutiny
    → Regulatory consequences in regulated sectors (CQC, FCA, Ofsted)
    → Follow-up compliance visits by Immigration Enforcement
    → Internal operational disruption during investigation
sections.keyPrinciple: A civil penalty is not a fine-and-forget event. It is the start of an escalation cascade that can destroy the employer's ability to recruit internationally, damage its reputation, and expose its directors to personal criminal liability. The rational employer treats right to work compliance as a core business risk — not a peripheral HR task.
refs: Immigration Rules Part 6A (sponsor licence framework)
Home Office Sponsor Guidance (sponsor compliance and licence management)
Company Directors Disqualification Act 1986 (disqualification following conviction)
Home Office quarterly penalty list (published naming)
Sector-specific regulators: CQC (care), FCA (financial services), Ofsted (education)
commonErrors: Treating a civil penalty as an isolated financial matter without assessing the sponsor licence implications — for licensed sponsors, the financial penalty is often the LEAST significant consequence
Failing to take immediate remedial action after a civil penalty — the Home Office conducts follow-up visits. Employers who cannot demonstrate improved compliance systems face escalating sanctions
Not informing the board or senior management about a civil penalty — the escalation cascade can affect the entire business. Board-level awareness is essential for strategic response
Assuming that a revoked sponsor licence can be easily replaced — there is a 12-month cooling-off period before the employer can reapply. During this period, no sponsored workers can be employed

pendingAmendments.status: Since 2025, Immigration Enforcement has referred more cases for criminal investigation where employers repeatedly breach right to work rules. Monitor for published enforcement data and policy statements on criminal prosecution thresholds.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — the boundary between civil and criminal enforcement is shifting towards more criminal prosecutions

pendingAmendments.status: The Home Office is transitioning from physical immigration documents (BRPs) to eVisas. Employers must adapt their right to work checking processes to use the online service rather than relying on physical documents. Monitor for updated guidance on acceptable check methods.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: HIGH — employers relying on manual document checks for visa holders may lose statutory excuse if they fail to transition to online checks

pendingAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 — Fair Work Agency enforcement
pendingAmendments.status: The ERA 2025 establishes the Fair Work Agency which may coordinate with Immigration Enforcement. Monitor for interactions between labour market enforcement and right to work compliance.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — potential for coordinated enforcement across immigration and employment law
